g.data-narration: Welcome to Multiversion Concurrency Control. This technique revolutionizes how databases handle concurrent access to data.
Traditional locking causes blocking. Read operations must wait for exclusive locks held by writers, reducing throughput.
g.text: This blocking leads to reduced throughput and potential deadlocks.

g.data-narration: MVCC solves this by maintaining multiple data versions. Readers access old snapshots while writers create new versions.
Each data version stores metadata: Transaction ID, begin/end timestamps, and pointers linking version chains.
g.text: Each version contains metadata enabling the database to select the appropriate version based on transaction timestamp.

g.data-narration: Timestamp ordering assigns unique timestamps to transactions. Operations are ordered by these timestamps to ensure serializability.
g.text: Transactions ordered by timestamp determine serialization order.

g.data-narration: The Read Rule allows reading if transaction timestamp is newer than the last write. The Write Rule requires newer timestamps than both last read and write.
g.text: T can read Q if: TS(T) > WriteTS(Q)
Transaction must be newer than last writer
T can write Q if: TS(T) > ReadTS(Q) AND TS(T) > WriteTS(Q)
Transaction must be newer than all
If rules violated, transaction restarts with new timestamp.

g.data-narration: Thomas's Write Rule optimizes by ignoring obsolete writes. If a transaction tries to write but a newer write exists, the old write can be safely skipped.
g.text: Since T2's write is newer, T1's write would be overwritten anyway. Skip it to improve throughput.

g.data-narration: Snapshot Isolation provides each transaction a consistent database view from transaction start time. All reads see committed data only.
g.text: T1 sees a consistent snapshot throughout its execution, unaffected by concurrent transactions.

g.data-narration: In Snapshot Isolation, write-write conflicts are resolved by First-Committer-Wins. The first transaction to commit succeeds; others abort.
Multi-Version Timestamp Ordering ensures reads never block or abort. They simply read the appropriate version based on timestamp.
g.text: In MVTO, read operations never block and never abort. They simply find and read the appropriate version.

g.data-narration: SSI extends Snapshot Isolation to provide true serializability. It detects dangerous structures in transaction dependency graphs.
g.text: SSI detects write skew and other serialization anomalies, aborting one conflicting transaction to ensure serializability.

g.data-narration: This example shows MVCC in action: a long-running read query completes successfully while concurrent updates happen.
g.text: T1 reads old version ($50K), T2 creates new version ($55K), T3 sees new version - all without any blocking!

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates write-write conflict resolution in Snapshot Isolation using the First-Committer-Wins rule.
